title: Patch file inside running containers file system
body:
Patch file with a given patch inside running containers

User need to give:
- the full path to the file to patch inside the running containers
- the full patch of the patch file to apply inside the running containers

User can patch all the running containers by pass the `--all` flag.

User can ignore case where the file to patch doesn't exist by pass the `ignore-fail`.

Usage:
podman patch [options] <file-to-patch> <patch-file> [<container>]

Example:
- podman patch /etc/profile /tmp/profile.patch cont1 cont2 cont3
- podman patch /etc/profile /tmp/profile.patch --all

Signed-off-by: Hervé Beraud <hberaud@redhat.com>

Hi @4383. Thanks for your PR.

I'm waiting for a [containers](https://github.com/orgs/containers/people) member to verify that this patch is reasonable to test. If it is, they should reply with `/ok-to-test` on its own line. Until that is done, I will not automatically test new commits in this PR, but the usual testing commands by org members will still work. Regular contributors should [join the org](https://github.com/orgs/containers/people) to skip this step.

Once the patch is verified, the new status will be reflected by the `ok-to-test` label.

I understand the commands that are listed [here](https://go.k8s.io/bot-commands?repo=containers%2Flibpod).

<details>

Instructions for interacting with me using PR comments are available [here](https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/pull-requests.md).  If you have questions or suggestions related to my behavior, please file an issue against the [kubernetes/test-infra](https://github.com/kubernetes/test-infra/issues/new?title=Prow%20issue:) repository.
</details>
Looks like there's an extra import in the tests?

I think @mheon hit most of the biggies and I hit a few other things.  @rhatdan / @mheon   have you guys seen folks asking for this type of function?

does anyone know if silverblue ships patch by default? if not, we'd be shipping a command that would fail by default on there. is there an established golang library that could do the patch without the need for the patch command?
i defer to @rhatdan on this one.

First I am not sure what the purpose of this command is, There is no Docker equivalent?
Secondly we don't want this as a primary command, if we are going to accept it, it should be
podman container patch.

Missing completions.

Needs update to commands.go transfer.go.

I think we should not suggest (and support) to patch live containers.

We suggest to use read only images, and in cases when you really need to modify the container file system, we already have a more flexible way with `podman mount $ID`

@4383 I think that using "podman mount" is more flexible than having "patch" for a very specific use case.

With "podman mount", your example will look like:
```
for ctr in cont1 cont2 cont3; do
    mount=$(podman mount $ctr)
    patch -d $(mount) < foo.diff
    podman umount $ctr
done
````

> @4383 I think that using "podman mount" is more flexible than having "patch" for a very specific use case.
> 
> With "podman mount", your example will look like:
> 
> ```
> for ctr in cont1 cont2 cont3; do
>     mount=$(podman mount $ctr)
>     patch -d $(mount) < foo.diff
>     podman umount $ctr
> done
> ```

I concur with @giuseppe. Although I find the idea of this PR cool, I see it as a use-case of `podman-mount` (rather than a new command) which can be used transparently via, for instance, bash as shown above. There are countless ways of altering a container's file system and using `patch` is just one of them.
I agree.  Thanks @4383 for the effort, but I think it is better just to build it around podman mount.

@rhatdan @vrothberg @giuseppe no problems this is why I ask you to choose if we continue with it or stop it :)

Thanks for replies.
